overview: This first chunk is the opening משנה of מסכת ברכות. It asks when evening קריאת שמע begins, gives the starting point, then presents three views about the ending time, followed by a story and a rule that explains how to understand the חכמים when they say "until midnight."

steps.whatsHappening: The משנה opens the מסכת by defining the beginning time of evening קריאת שמע. It is starting with the basic time frame before discussing the latest time.
steps.simpleTranslation: From when does one recite שמע in the evening? From the time that the כהנים enter to eat their תרומה.

phrases.english: From when does one recite Shema
phrases.notes: Opening question of the משנה.
Specifies the nighttime שמע.
phrases.english: in the evening?
From the time that the priests enter
phrases.notes: Beginning time marker.

phrases.english: to eat their teruma.
phrases.notes: The practical סימן for the start time.

steps.title: The משנה opens with the time for evening שמע
steps.stepSummary: The משנה begins by asking from when one says evening קריאת שמע, and gives the starting time as when כהנים may begin eating תרומה.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Instead of saying a clock time, the משנה uses a הלכתי marker: when כהנים become permitted to eat תרומה. That means the learner must notice that the משנה is tying one law to another law's timing.

keyTerms.meaning: from when; starting at what time

steps.mimraCoreRuling: The beginning time of evening קריאת שמע is when כהנים may eat תרומה.

steps.whatsHappening: The משנה now moves from the starting time to the ending time. It presents the first opinion, that of רבי אליעזר.
steps.simpleTranslation: Until the end of the first watch. These are the words of רבי אליעזר.

phrases.notes: Latest time according to רבי אליעזר.
phrases.english: Until the end of the first watch.
These are the words of Rabbi Eliezer.
phrases.notes: Attribution of the opinion.

steps.title: Rabbi Eliezer gives the latest time
steps.stepSummary: The משנה now gives רבי אליעזר's view: evening קריאת שמע may be said only until the end of the first watch of the night.
steps.mimraCoreRuling: According to רבי אליעזר, evening קריאת שמע ends at the first watch.

keyTerms.meaning: the end of the first watch of the night
these are the words of רבי אליעזר

steps.deeperAnalysis: This is a narrower end time than the full night. The משנה is building a three-way dispute about how long the nighttime mitzvah lasts.
steps.relationToPreviousStep: Gives the first ending-time opinion after the opening start time.

steps.title: The Rabbis set the time until midnight
steps.stepSummary: The משנה presents the view of חכמים that evening קריאת שמע may be recited until midnight.
steps.deeperAnalysis: חכמים extend the time beyond רבי אליעזר's first watch, but not as far as dawn. The sugya will later need to explain whether midnight is the real deadline or a protective limit.
steps.mimraCoreRuling: According to חכמים, evening קריאת שמע is until midnight.
steps.relationToPreviousStep: Adds a second opinion with a later deadline.
steps.whatsHappening: The משנה gives a second opinion about the latest time. This is a middle position between an early cutoff and the whole night.
steps.simpleTranslation: And the Rabbis say: Until midnight.

phrases.english: And the Rabbis say:
phrases.notes: Introduces the second opinion.
Their stated deadline.
phrases.english: Until midnight.

phrases.notes: Introduces the third opinion.
phrases.english: Rabban Gamliel says
until dawn rises.
phrases.notes: His latest time is dawn.

steps.simpleTranslation: Rabban Gamliel says: Until the dawn rises.
steps.whatsHappening: The משנה now gives the broadest opinion about the latest time. It is completing the three-way dispute.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבן גמליאל treats the mitzvah as lasting through the night until dawn. The next story will show this opinion in practice and explain its relationship to חכמים.
steps.mimraCoreRuling: According to רבן גמליאל, evening קריאת שמע may be recited until dawn.

keyTerms.meaning: dawn; the rise of first light

steps.stepSummary: The משנה gives a third opinion: רבן גמליאל says the time lasts until dawn.
steps.relationToPreviousStep: Adds the third and latest deadline.
steps.title: Rabban Gamliel allows until dawn

steps.relationToPreviousStep: Moves from abstract opinions to a practical case.
steps.raayaObject: How late one may still recite evening קריאת שמע.
steps.stepSummary: The משנה brings a story: רבן גמליאל's sons came back late from a feast and told him they had not yet said קריאת שמע.
steps.raayaSupportSource: A story involving רבן גמליאל and his sons.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The sons are in a case of delay: it is very late, and they are unsure whether the mitzvah time has passed. The story is not just narrative; it is evidence for how to apply the time dispute.

keyTerms.meaning: an incident; a story used as a case
a banquet hall; house of feasting

steps.title: A story about Rabban Gamliel’s sons returning late

phrases.english: There was an incident, and his sons came
phrases.notes: Begins the story.

phrases.english: from the house of feasting,
phrases.notes: They returned late from a celebration.

phrases.english: they said to him:
phrases.notes: Introduces their question/report.

phrases.english: We did not recite Shema,
phrases.notes: They have not yet fulfilled the mitzvah.

steps.whatsHappening: The משנה is now bringing a story to set up how רבן גמליאל's view works in real life. This story prepares the next ruling.
steps.simpleTranslation: There was an incident: His sons came from the house of feasting. They said to him: We did not recite Shema.

steps.title: He tells them they still must recite Shema before dawn
steps.deeperAnalysis: This applies his earlier opinion to the sons' case. The key change is from uncertainty to a clear instruction: the obligation is still active before עמוד השחר.
steps.stepSummary: רבן גמליאל answers that if dawn has not yet arrived, they are still obligated to recite קריאת שמע.
steps.whatsHappening: רבן גמליאל answers the practical case directly. He rules that the mitzvah still remains as long as it is before dawn.
steps.simpleTranslation: He said to them: If dawn has not yet risen, you are obligated to recite.
steps.teshuvahAnswerProvided: Before dawn, the obligation of evening קריאת שמע still remains according to רבן גמליאל.

phrases.notes: Introduces the ruling.
phrases.english: He said to them:

phrases.notes: Condition for the ruling.
phrases.english: If dawn has not yet risen

phrases.notes: They must still say שמע.
phrases.english: you are obligated to recite.

steps.relationToPreviousStep: Generalizes the story into a broader concluding rule.
steps.confusionAlert: Do not say that חכמים hold the mitzvah is inherently only until midnight. In this משנה's closing line, midnight is presented as the stated cutoff, while the mitzvah itself still lasts until עמוד השחר.
steps.whyThisMatters: This closing rule becomes a model for reading many time limits in חז"ל: sometimes the stated deadline is a protective boundary, while the underlying mitzvah window is longer.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This step explains the relationship between רבן גמליאל and חכמים: חכמים's wording does not erase the full-night mitzvah window. The practical statement of midnight functions as a protective deadline, while the core mitzvah time continues until עמוד השחר.

keyTerms.meaning: their mitzvah; the time for fulfilling them

steps.stepSummary: The משנה concludes that this is not only true for שמע. In every case where חכמים said "until midnight," the mitzvah really lasts until dawn.
steps.whatToRemember: In this משנה, the practical headline is: evening קריאת שמע starts when כהנים may eat תרומה. For the latest time, רבי אליעזר says first watch, חכמים say midnight, and רבן גמליאל says dawn; then the משנה explains that whenever חכמים say "until midnight," the mitzvah itself extends until dawn.
steps.title: Midnight is not the final mitzvah deadline
steps.maskanaFinalTakeaway: When חכמים say "until midnight," the mitzvah itself extends until dawn.

phrases.english: And not only this did they say,
phrases.notes: Expands beyond the case of שמע.
General rule about similar cases.
phrases.english: but rather wherever the Rabbis said

phrases.notes: The formula under discussion.
phrases.english: "until midnight,"

phrases.notes: Real mitzvah window continues.
phrases.english: their mitzvah is until there rises
the dawn.
phrases.notes: Final endpoint.

steps.whatsHappening: The משנה now states the broader rule behind the story. It is concluding that "until midnight" said by חכמים is a limit they stated, but the mitzvah itself lasts until dawn.
steps.simpleTranslation: And not only this did they say, but rather wherever the Rabbis said: Until midnight, their mitzva is until dawn rises.

steps.title: The משנה generalizes the rule to other mitzvot
steps.triggerLanguage: אִם כֵּן, לָמָה אָמְרוּ חֲכָמִים ... כְּדֵי
steps.whatToRemember: A major יסוד of this משנה: when חז״ל say "until midnight" here, they are often giving a protective deadline, while the core mitzvah-time still runs until dawn.
steps.whyThisMatters: This line gives the reason for the whole rabbinic cutoff. It teaches how חז״ל build protective fences around a Torah mitzvah-time.

keyTerms.meaning: burning the fats and limbs of offerings on the מזבח
offerings that may be eaten for one day
to distance a person from sin

steps.deeperAnalysis: This step explains the pattern behind the earlier case of קריאת שמע. The real mitzvah-time lasts until dawn, but חז״ל moved the practical deadline earlier so a person will not delay and miss it.
steps.stepSummary: The משנה says that burning sacrificial fats and limbs, and offerings eaten for one day, really remain valid until dawn. It then explains that חז״ל’s limit of midnight was only a protective fence.
steps.relationToPreviousStep: The משנה now closes the discussion by explaining the broader rule behind רבן גמליאל’s answer.
steps.whatsHappening: The משנה finishes its larger point: "until midnight" is not the real Torah deadline in these cases. It is a safety rule made by חז״ל.
steps.simpleTranslation: The burning of fats and limbs is valid until dawn, and all offerings eaten for one day are also valid until dawn. If so, why did חז״ל say until midnight? To distance a person from sin.

phrases.english: The burning of fats and limbs,
phrases.notes: A sacrificial service done on the altar.
This is the true endpoint.
phrases.english: their mitzvah lasts until dawn rises
and all offerings that are eaten for one day,
phrases.notes: Another example category.

phrases.english: their mitzvah lasts until dawn rises.
phrases.notes: Again, the real deadline is dawn.
The משנה now asks its own explanatory question.
phrases.english: If so, why did the Sages say
"until midnight"
phrases.notes: The earlier rabbinic limit.
The reason for the fence.
phrases.english: — in order to distance a person from transgression.

steps.maskanaFinalTakeaway: In these cases, the real mitzvah-time lasts until dawn; "until midnight" is a protective rabbinic fence.

phrases.english: Where is the tanna standing
phrases.notes: Meaning: what prior context is he assuming?

phrases.english: that he teaches, "From when"?
phrases.notes: Why begin with a timing question?

steps.simpleTranslation: The גמרא asks: Where is the תנא standing, that he teaches "From when"?
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא is starting a new analysis of the very first משנה. It asks why the משנה opens as if the topic was already introduced earlier.
steps.relationToPreviousStep: After the משנה ends, the גמרא now opens its analysis of the wording of the first משנה.

keyTerms.meaning: where is he standing; what prior basis is he assuming

steps.deeperAnalysis: "מֵאֵימָתַי" usually sounds like timing details of something already established. But this is the opening משנה, so the גמרא wants to know why the תנא chose that style.
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא opens by asking what earlier discussion the תנא is standing on when he says "מֵאֵימָתַי." That wording sounds like the basic obligation was already known.
steps.title: Why does the first משנה say "From when"?
steps.sheelahInformationSought: Why the first משנה uses wording that sounds like it is continuing an already-known obligation.

steps.deeperAnalysis: Even if evening קריאת שמע is a valid place to start, the גמרא still wants to know why it comes before morning קריאת שמע. The order itself needs explanation.

keyTerms.meaning: what is different; why specifically this one

steps.stepSummary: The גמרא adds a second opening question: why teach ערבית first? It seems more natural to start with שחרית.
steps.relationToPreviousStep: The גמרא strengthens its opening analysis with a second question about the משנה’s order.
steps.title: Why does the משנה start with evening before morning?
steps.sheelahInformationSought: Why the תנא chose to begin the tractate with evening קריאת שמע rather than morning קריאת שמע.

phrases.notes: A second question is being added.
phrases.english: And furthermore: What is different

phrases.notes: Why begin with the night-time law?
phrases.english: that he taught evening first?
Let him teach morning first!
phrases.notes: The suggested more natural order.

steps.simpleTranslation: And furthermore: What is different about evening, that he taught it first? Let him teach morning first.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא adds another structural question about the first משנה. It is still setting up the opening problem before giving one answer to both questions.

steps.terutzHavaAmina: Without this answer, the משנה looked like it assumed prior discussion and chose an odd order.
steps.whatToRemember: The first משנה is arranged by the פסוק: first "בְּשָׁכְבְּךָ," then "וּבְקוּמֶךָ." That is why it opens with evening קריאת שמע and asks when that night-time period begins.
steps.terutzResolutionType: Reframing the משנה as following the order and language of a פסוק.
steps.title: The תנא follows the order of the פסוק
steps.relationToPreviousStep: The גמרא now resolves both opening questions with one verse-based explanation.
steps.whyThisMatters: This answer shows that the structure of the משנה is not random. The Oral Torah is being organized to match the Torah’s own wording.
steps.confusionAlert: Do not read the גמרא as saying the obligation of קריאת שמע was unknown before the משנה. The question is only why the משנה chose this opening wording and order.
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא answers both questions together: the תנא is based on the פסוק "בְּשָׁכְבְּךָ וּבְקוּמֶךָ." So he starts with the time of lying down, meaning evening קריאת שמע.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Once the תנא is read as building directly from "בְּשָׁכְבְּךָ וּבְקוּמֶךָ," both problems disappear. "מֵאֵימָתַי" means: when does the Torah’s "lying down" time begin, and that is why evening comes first.

keyTerms.meaning: he is based on the verse
when you lie down and when you rise
the time of קריאת שמע of lying down
the priests enter to eat their תרומה

steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא now gives one answer to both earlier questions. The משנה starts with evening because the תנא is following the Torah’s wording in the פסוק.
steps.simpleTranslation: The תנא is standing on the verse, as it is written: "when you lie down and when you rise." And this is what he teaches: When is the time of the recitation of שמע of lying down? From the time that the priests enter to eat their תרומה.
steps.terutzMaskana: The משנה is intentionally built from "בְּשָׁכְבְּךָ וּבְקוּמֶךָ," so evening comes first and "מֵאֵימָתַי" asks when the Torah’s lying-down time begins.
steps.teshuvahAnswerProvided: The תנא is following the פסוק and therefore opens with the time of evening קריאת שמע: from when the כהנים enter to eat תרומה.

phrases.notes: This answers the structural questions.
phrases.english: The tanna is based on the verse,
as it is written: "when you lie down and when you rise."
phrases.notes: The verse gives the order: night first, then day.

phrases.english: And this is what he teaches:
phrases.notes: The גמרא now paraphrases the משנה.

phrases.english: When is the time for the recitation of Shema of lying down?
phrases.notes: This explains the meaning of "מֵאֵימָתַי."
Beginning of the practical answer.
phrases.english: — From the time that the priests enter
to eat their teruma.
phrases.notes: The known marker for the start of the evening time.

steps.simpleTranslation: And if you want, say: he learns it from the creation of the world, as it is written: “And there was evening and there was morning, one day.”
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא is giving an additional answer to the earlier question about why ערבית comes first. It says the order follows the way the Torah describes creation.
steps.teshuvahAnswerProvided: The תנא starts with evening because the Torah’s description of creation also places evening before morning.

phrases.notes: Introduces an alternate answer, not a rejection of the first one.
phrases.english: And if you want, say:

phrases.notes: A second basis for putting evening first.
phrases.english: he derives it from the creation of the world,
as it is written:
phrases.notes: Introduces the verse.

phrases.english: “And there was evening and there was morning, one day.”
phrases.notes: The verse presents evening before morning.

steps.title: A second reason to start with evening
steps.relationToPreviousStep: The גמרא adds a second answer to the opening question instead of relying only on the earlier פסוק.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Until now, the answer was based on the פסוק of "בְּשָׁכְבְּךָ וּבְקוּמֶךָ." Now the גמרא adds a second support: the world itself is described as evening first, then morning. So evening-first is not random; it matches a Torah pattern.

keyTerms.meaning: and if you want, say; an alternate answer
from the creation of the world

steps.stepSummary: The גמרא offers another answer for why the תנא began with evening קריאת שמע: the order follows creation, where evening comes before morning.

steps.title: A later משנה seems to list morning first
steps.kashyaAttackLogic: If evening-first is the organizing principle, the later משנה about the ברכות should also have listed ערבית before שחרית.
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא challenges this evening-first logic from the later משנה about the ברכות of קריאת שמע, where morning is listed before evening.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This is a real test of consistency. If the תנא is carefully following an evening-first order, that pattern should appear later too. Since it does not, the גמרא asks whether the earlier explanation is enough.

keyTerms.meaning: let it teach / it should teach

steps.relationToPreviousStep: The גמרא tests the new explanation against a later part of the משנה.
steps.kashyaTarget: The claim that the תנא consistently gives evening priority because evening comes first.
steps.simpleTranslation: If so, the latter clause that teaches: “In the morning he recites two before it and one after it; in the evening he recites two before it and two after it” — it should have taught evening first!
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא pushes back on the evening-first idea from another part of the משנה. If evening really has priority, why does that later line discuss שחרית before ערבית?

phrases.english: If so,
phrases.notes: Opens the challenge based on the previous answer.
Refers to a later משנה passage.
phrases.english: the latter clause that teaches

phrases.notes: Beginning of the quoted משנה.
phrases.english: “In the morning he recites two blessings before it
and one after it,
phrases.notes: Completes the morning side.

phrases.english: in the evening he recites two blessings before it
phrases.notes: Now the evening side is quoted.

phrases.english: and two after it,”
phrases.notes: Completes the evening side.

phrases.english: it should have taught evening first!
phrases.notes: The challenge: the order there seems reversed.

steps.whatToRemember: A תנא may keep one big opening order, but still arrange later details by topic flow.
steps.terutzHavaAmina: We assumed that if evening is first, every later list in the משנה must also put evening first.
steps.title: The תנא followed topic flow, not strict order everywhere
steps.terutzResolutionType: distinguishing between overall sequence and local topic development
steps.relationToPreviousStep: This answers the challenge by distinguishing between the opening order and the order of later details.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The answer is that there are two different kinds of order: opening order and local discussion order. The תנא honored evening-first at the start, but later, once שחרית was already on the table, he completed that topic before circling back to ערבית.

keyTerms.meaning: once he was standing in / occupied with

steps.stepSummary: The גמרא answers that the תנא did begin with ערבית, then moved to שחרית, and once he was already discussing שחרית he finished those details before returning to ערבית.
steps.simpleTranslation: The תנא opened with evening, and then taught morning. Once he was standing in morning, he explained the matters of morning, and then explained the matters of evening.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא defends the משנה’s later order. It says the תנא was following the flow of the discussion once he had already moved into שחרית.
steps.terutzMaskana: The תנא began with evening overall, but later he finished the שחרית discussion once he was already there before returning to ערבית.

phrases.notes: Affirms the original evening-first structure.
phrases.english: The תנא opened with evening,
and then taught morning,
phrases.notes: He next moved to שחרית.
This is the key transition logic.
phrases.english: once he was already dealing with morning,
he explained the matters of morning,
phrases.notes: He completed the current topic.

phrases.english: and then explained the matters of evening.
phrases.notes: Only afterward did he return to ערבית details.

phrases.english: The Master said
phrases.notes: Standard formula introducing a quoted line from the משנה.
Quotes the opening time marker.
phrases.english: from the time that the priests enter

phrases.notes: Completes the משנה’s wording.
phrases.english: to eat their תרומה.

phrases.notes: The גמרא asks for the practical time behind the sign.
phrases.english: Now then, when do priests eat תרומה?
phrases.aramaic: מִכְּדִי כֹּהֲנִים אֵימַת קָא אָכְלִי תְּרוּמָה?

phrases.notes: States the known time explicitly.
phrases.english: From the time of the emergence of the stars,

phrases.notes: The challenge is about why the משנה chose indirect wording.
phrases.english: let it teach: “From the time of the emergence of the stars”!

steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא is moving to a new question on the משנה’s wording. It asks why the time for evening קריאת שמע is described indirectly through כהנים and תרומה.
steps.simpleTranslation: The Master said: From the time that the priests enter to eat their תרומה. Now then, when do priests eat תרומה? From the time of the emergence of the stars. Let it teach: “From the time of the emergence of the stars”!
steps.relationToPreviousStep: After finishing the order question, the גמרא begins a new close reading of the משנה’s opening phrase.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is the גמרא’s first example of a common move: not asking what the law is, but why the משנה chose a particular way to say it.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא is not doubting the actual זמן. It accepts that the time is צֵאת הַכּוֹכָבִים and is asking only why the משנה chose this wording.
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא now starts analyzing the opening line of the משנה itself. It asks why the משנה uses the indirect sign of כהנים eating תרומה instead of simply naming the time as צֵאת הַכּוֹכָבִים.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא assumes that כהנים eating תרומה begins at a known time: צֵאת הַכּוֹכָבִים. If so, the משנה seems unnecessarily roundabout. This sets up the next move, where the גמרא will explain what the indirect wording adds.

keyTerms.meaning: the Master said; the גמרא quotes the משנה for analysis

steps.sheelahInformationSought: Why the משנה defines the start time through כהנים eating תרומה instead of directly saying צֵאת הַכּוֹכָבִים.
steps.title: Why define the time through כהנים eating תרומה

steps.terutzResolutionType: The wording is justified because it teaches an extra הלכה.
steps.title: The משנה teaches an extra law through its wording
steps.terutzHavaAmina: We thought the משנה should have used the simpler direct time expression.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is the first place the גמרא shows that a wording choice in a משנה may carry hidden legal information, not just style.

keyTerms.meaning: an extra point taught along the way
the atonement offering that completes purification

steps.deeperAnalysis: So the wording is not random and not just poetic. By mentioning כהנים eating תרומה, the משנה quietly tells you both the time marker and the rule that only sunset-nightfall matters here, not later כַּפָּרָה.
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא answers that the משנה could have said צֵאת הַכּוֹכָבִים directly, but instead used כהנים eating תרומה to teach an extra point: כהנים begin then, and כַּפָּרָה is not required before eating תרומה.
steps.relationToPreviousStep: This directly answers why the משנה did not simply say צֵאת הַכּוֹכָבִים.
steps.terutzMaskana: The משנה intentionally used כהנים eating תרומה to teach both the time and that כַּפָּרָה does not delay eating תרומה.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא now answers the question from the previous step. It explains that the משנה used an indirect sign on purpose, because that wording teaches another הלכה too.
steps.simpleTranslation: The wording was chosen to teach something additional. The time when כהנים may eat תרומה is from צֵאת הַכּוֹכָבִים, and this also teaches that bringing the atonement offering does not hold them back from eating תרומה.

phrases.english: It teaches us an additional matter along the way
phrases.notes: The גמרא says the wording has a side lesson built into it.
This is the extra topic the משנה's wording lets us learn.
phrases.english: When do priests eat teruma
From the time the stars emerge
phrases.notes: This identifies the actual time marker.
The גמרא adds a second lesson from the same wording.
phrases.english: And this also teaches us

phrases.notes: Even before bringing the required offering, he may already eat תרומה once night has come.
phrases.english: That atonement does not hold him back

steps.teshuvahAnswerProvided: The indirect wording was chosen to include an additional legal teaching.

steps.stepSummary: The גמרא brings a ברייתא on the פסוק 'וּבָא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וְטָהֵר' to support the rule. The ברייתא explains that sunset is what delays eating תרומה, but כַּפָּרָה is not what delays it.
steps.raayaSupportSource: A ברייתא interpreting the verse וּבָא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וְטָהֵר.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This is important because the previous step said the משנה hints to this law. Now the גמרא shows that the law is real and text-based: waiting for evening is essential, but waiting for the offering is not.

keyTerms.meaning: as it was taught in a ברייתא
and the sun sets and it is purified
the setting of his sun; arrival of evening

steps.relationToPreviousStep: This supports the extra law mentioned in the previous answer.
steps.raayaObject: That כַּפָּרָה does not delay a כהן from eating תרומה.
steps.title: A ברייתא supports that כַּפָּרָה does not delay תרומה

phrases.english: As it was taught in a baraita:
phrases.notes: This introduces supporting source material.
The ברייתא begins from the verse.
phrases.english: "And the sun sets and it is purified"
The setting of his sun prevents him
phrases.notes: He cannot eat before evening arrives.

phrases.english: from eating teruma
phrases.notes: This completes the first half of the rule.
The offering is not the delaying factor for תרומה.
phrases.english: But his atonement does not prevent him

phrases.notes: This repeats the practical result.
phrases.english: from eating teruma

steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא now backs up its answer with a source. It brings a ברייתא that states the same rule explicitly from a פסוק.
steps.simpleTranslation: As taught in a ברייתא: 'And the sun sets and it is purified' means that the setting of his sun prevents him from eating תרומה, but his atonement does not prevent him from eating תרומה.

steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא is not rejecting the rule; it is now checking the proof carefully. It asks how the ברייתא knows to read the words of the פסוק that way.
steps.simpleTranslation: And from where do we know that 'and the sun sets' means the setting of the sun, and that 'and it is purified' means the day becomes pure?

phrases.english: And from where do we know
phrases.notes: This opens a question on the proof-text reading.

phrases.english: that this phrase 'and the sun sets'
phrases.notes: The גמרא isolates the first phrase of the verse.

phrases.english: means the setting of the sun
phrases.notes: Meaning actual sunset leading into night.
Now the גמרא isolates the second phrase.
phrases.english: and this phrase 'and it is purified'

phrases.notes: That is, the day ended and the sky cleared into night.
phrases.english: means the day became pure

steps.title: How do we know the verse means sunset and the day becoming pure
steps.sheelahInformationSought: What textual basis proves that the verse refers to sunset and the day ending, rather than some other kind of purification.
steps.whatToRemember: So far the גמרא's answer is: the משנה used כהנים eating תרומה to teach two things at once. Then it brought a ברייתא to support that lack of כַּפָּרָה does not delay תרומה, and now it begins to analyze that ברייתא's reading of the פסוק.
steps.confusionAlert: Do not confuse 'וְטָהֵר' here with the person becoming fully pure through כַּפָּרָה. The גמרא is asking whether the word might instead mean that the day has cleared and ended.

keyTerms.meaning: and from where do we know
the day became pure; the day ended

steps.deeperAnalysis: This is a classic next move in the גמרא: after bringing a source, it tests the source-reading itself. The issue is not the law yet, but whether the words of the פסוק really support that law.
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא now questions the ברייתא's reading of the פסוק. How do we know 'וּבָא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ' means sunset, and 'וְטָהֵר' means the day has become pure?
steps.relationToPreviousStep: After citing the ברייתא, the גמרא now probes how that verse was interpreted.

mainTopic: The opening משנה sets the time range for the evening קריאת שמע and records three opinions about its latest time.
